memory.summary: I have an afternoon free, but starting the term project now is risky given my short planning horizon. A quick chore could be a win, though leisure feels like procrastination.
Two hours wasted on a low-impact task left me with only 0.28 progress; I'm now staring at the project start while my energy and sleep debt bleed out.
Two hours yielded 0.47 progress, but my energy and sleep debt are critically low. I need to finish the project immediately before the deadline.
Three hours finally pushed my project to 68%, but I'm burning out. With only two days left and zero sleep, I need to sprint now or fail.
I crushed the project to 76% in two hours, but ignoring that trivial chore was a mistake. I need to fix my priorities immediately before burning out.
Two hours just jumped progress to 83%, proving I can sprint hard when needed. But my energy is zero and stress is high; I need to stop ignoring this until the deadline.
Progress jumped to 87% in one hour, but my energy is zero and stress is spiking. I need to stop ignoring this sprint or fail.
I pushed the project to 95% in two hours, but my energy is zero and stress is spiking. I can't ignore this until the deadline.
Sprinting to 100% left me drained and stressed, but the deadline is only three days out. I need to stop ignoring this until then.
Finally slept enough to clear my debt, but I'm still drained from yesterday's sprint. Time to shift focus back to coursework before the deadline hits.
That trivial win feels hollow compared to the massive project sprint I just endured. My energy is critically low, but I need to force some focus back on coursework before the deadline.
This tiny win feels pathetic after my sprint. I need to force focus on the actual coursework before the deadline crushes me.

desires: Get that trivial chore done now so I can stop wasting mental energy on it before the real deadline hits.
Force myself to focus on the term project immediately because doing nothing else feels like a failure of my own standards.
Avoid feeling pathetic or incompetent tomorrow when I have to explain this 'tiny win' to my cohort.

goals.description: Finish the trivial chore immediately to claim the dopamine win and clear mental clutter before the term project deadline.
goals.rationale: I already wasted 60 minutes of my sleep-debt hours on this. My impulsive drive is screaming for a 'win' before I do anything else. Carrying around unfinished soft tasks creates mental clutter that bleeds into my sleep debt hours later. I need to close this loop immediately to assert control over my time and secure my standing as someone who executes, not just plans. The term project is locked; there's no room for distraction.

goals.description: Force immediate focus on the term project sprint to meet the Thursday deadline without further delay.
goals.rationale: Doing nothing else feels like a failure of my own standards. With only 3 days left and zero progress, I cannot afford any more procrastination bias. My value hierarchy prioritizes academic outcomes above all else; I must treat this grind as a necessary sacrifice to justify the burnout later.

goals.description: Skip the meal with cohort_3 tonight to preserve physical stamina for the upcoming sprint.
goals.rationale: I've already maximized my short-term gains (the chore) and stabilized my mental state (the reframing). Adding social obligations now risks draining the physical stamina I need for Thursday's sprint. My value hierarchy prioritizes academic outcomes and growth over leisure right now; I will drop this until my energy reserves recover or the deadline shifts.

While the chore gave a dopamine win, ignoring the sprint now risks total failure. I'll push hard for immediate gains to assert control, accepting the energy cost as a necessary sacrifice.
